tools.name: Buzzsprout
tools.short_description: Buzzsprout is a podcast hosting platform offering directory distribution, analytics, transcription, and monetization tools for independent podcasters.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Buzzsprout is a podcast hosting and distribution platform founded in 2009 by co-founders Tom Rossi and Kevin Finn and headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. The company has grown into one of the most widely used independent podcast hosts, reported to have around 53 employees, roughly 3,300+ paying customers, and approximately $5.6 million in revenue as of 2024, based on third-party revenue tracking. Buzzsprout hosts audio (and optionally video) episode files, generates a standards-compliant RSS feed, and automatically submits that feed to major podcast directories including Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and Amazon Music, removing the manual submission work podcasters would otherwise have to do themselves. The platform provides advanced download and listener analytics, automatic AI-generated episode transcripts, a customizable podcast website and embeddable/social-sharing episode players, monetization tools through an ad network and listener support/subscription features, custom RSS feed options, team collaboration for shows with multiple contributors, and audio processing tools. A newer Audio + Video plan lets podcasters publish video episodes directly to Apple Podcasts at up to 1080p resolution from the same platform used for audio distribution. Buzzsprout's pricing is usage-based around monthly upload hours rather than storage: a free plan allows 2 hours of uploads per month with episodes hosted for 90 days; the Audio plan is $15/month (billed annually) for 72 upload hours per year and one podcast; the Audio + Video plan is $25/month (annual billing) for the same upload allowance plus video publishing; the Multi-Podcast plan is $30/month (annual billing) and covers 5-10 shows sharing a pool of 180 upload hours per year; and a $5/month Archive plan keeps an inactive show's episodes live without allowing new uploads. All paid plans include automatic transcripts, directory distribution, custom RSS feeds, and team collaboration features. Buzzsprout is positioned for independent podcasters, small podcast networks, and businesses running branded podcasts who want hosting, distribution, monetization, and analytics in one platform without managing their own media server or manually submitting to each directory.

features.title: Automatic directory distribution
features.description: Submits the podcast RSS feed automatically to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other major directories.

features.title: Advanced analytics
features.description: Provides detailed download and listener data, including geographic and platform breakdowns, beyond basic download counts.

features.title: AI-generated transcripts
features.description: Automatically transcribes episodes, improving accessibility and giving podcasters repurposable text content.

features.title: Podcast website and players
features.description: Includes a customizable podcast website plus embeddable and social-media-optimized episode players.

features.title: Monetization tools
features.description: Offers an ad network marketplace and listener support/subscription options to help podcasters generate revenue.

features.title: Video podcast publishing
features.description: The Audio + Video plan lets creators publish video episodes directly to Apple Podcasts at up to 1080p resolution.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Supports multiple team members and co-hosts working on the same show from one account.

pros: Simple setup with automatic submission to all major podcast directories
Free plan available to try the platform before paying
Built-in AI transcripts and analytics without needing third-party tools
Video podcast publishing supported alongside traditional audio hosting
cons: Free plan limits uploads to 2 hours/month with only 90-day episode retention
Pricing is based on upload hours, which can be confusing compared to flat unlimited-storage competitors
Advanced monetization features depend on meeting ad network eligibility requirements
Higher-tier plans required for shows publishing many hours of content per month

features: Video publishing to Apple Podcasts up to 1080p
Keeps podcast live without new uploads

tools.name: Castos
tools.short_description: Castos is a podcast hosting platform for creators and agencies offering unlimited downloads, WordPress integration, YouTube republishing, and private podcasts.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Castos is a podcast hosting platform founded by Craig Hewitt around 2016 as a bootstrapped company that grew out of the WordPress podcasting plugin ecosystem. It offers unlimited podcast hosting with no storage caps or per-download overage fees, positioning itself against usage-limited competitors by charging a flat monthly rate per tier regardless of how many episodes or shows a customer publishes. Castos' four subscription tiers are Essentials at $19/month (up to 100 private subscribers, roughly 20,000 monthly downloads, and 10 monthly transcription credits), Growth at $49/month (up to 250 private subscribers, about 75,000 monthly downloads, 25 transcription credits, YouTube republishing, and Headliner integration for creating audiogram social clips), Pro at $99/month (up to 500 private subscribers, roughly 200,000 monthly downloads, 100 transcription credits, and video file hosting), and Castos Premium at $499/month, which adds custom contracts and invoicing, single sign-on, higher usage limits, and a dedicated account manager. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial. Castos' core differentiator is its deep WordPress integration: the company maintains the Seriously Simple Podcasting plugin, which lets podcasters publish episodes directly from their WordPress dashboard rather than a separate hosting-platform interface, and Castos also offers a dedicated hosting-for-agencies program for teams managing podcasts on behalf of multiple clients. Beyond WordPress publishing, Castos automatically distributes episodes to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, YouTube, and other major directories, provides detailed audience analytics covering listener location, device, and playback duration per episode, and includes a customizable podcast website builder that does not require design or development work. Castos also supports private podcast feeds, used by companies and course creators for internal communications, employee onboarding, and gated paid content distributed outside the public podcast directories. An AI Assistant feature automatically generates show notes, episode titles, social media posts, and chapter markers from uploaded audio, reducing post-production workload for solo creators and small teams. Castos operates as a fully remote, distributed company, which is reflected in inconsistent public records of a single legal headquarters. Castos is best suited to podcasters and podcasting agencies who want a hosting platform without download-based overage fees, who publish through WordPress, or who need private/internal podcast feeds — as opposed to creators who want the lowest possible entry price, where flat-rate competitors with cheaper starting tiers may be more cost-effective at very low download volumes.

features.title: Unlimited Podcast Hosting
features.description: Hosts unlimited shows and episodes with no storage caps and no per-download overage fees within plan limits.

features.title: WordPress Publishing (Seriously Simple Podcasting)
features.description: Publishes episodes directly from a WordPress dashboard using Castos' own WordPress plugin.

features.title: Multi-Platform Distribution
features.description: Automatically pushes new episodes to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, YouTube, and other major directories.

features.title: AI Assistant
features.description: Generates show notes, episode titles, social posts, and chapter markers automatically from uploaded audio.

features.title: Private Podcast Feeds
features.description: Distributes gated audio for internal communications, employee onboarding, courses, or paid memberships.

features.title: Audience Analytics
features.description: Reports listener location, device type, and playback duration per episode.

features.title: YouTube Republishing & Headliner Integration
features.description: Republishes episodes as YouTube videos and creates audiogram-style social clips on Growth tier and above.

pros: Flat monthly pricing with no per-download overage fees
Deep WordPress integration through the Seriously Simple Podcasting plugin
AI Assistant reduces manual show-notes and social-post production work
Supports private/internal podcast feeds in addition to public distribution
cons: Entry-tier download and transcription-credit limits may be restrictive for fast-growing shows
Video hosting and higher subscriber limits are gated behind the Pro and Premium tiers
Company headquarters and team structure are not clearly publicized, reflecting its fully remote operating model
Entry pricing is higher than some no-frills podcast hosts

tools.name: Podbean
tools.short_description: Podbean is a podcast hosting, distribution, and monetization platform that lets creators publish audio and video shows, track analytics, and earn revenue through ads, subscriptions, and patron support.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Podbean is a podcast hosting, distribution, and monetization platform founded in 2006 by David Xu, making it one of the longest-running dedicated podcast hosting services still operating. The company is bootstrapped without outside venture funding and reports hosting well over 125,000 active podcasts and billions of cumulative downloads. Podbean's core product lets creators upload audio or video episodes, generate a standards-compliant RSS feed, distribute automatically to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other directories with one click, and manage everything through a customizable podcast website and mobile recording app. Its main differentiator versus pure hosting tools like Buzzsprout or Libsyn is a built-in monetization suite: listener-supported Patron memberships, dynamic ad insertion through the Podbean Ad Marketplace, premium/paywalled content, and one-time listener donations, all managed from the same dashboard as hosting. Podbean also bundles AI-assisted tools, including AI-generated show notes, transcription, and audio enhancement, consumed via a monthly AI credit allowance. Pricing is freemium: a free plan offers roughly 5 hours of total storage and 100GB monthly bandwidth, while paid tiers (Unlimited Audio, Unlimited Plus, Network, and Business) scale from $12 to $99+ per month on annual billing and add unlimited storage, unmetered bandwidth, more podcast channels, team seats, and enterprise features like SSO and SOC2 controls. Target users span solo hobbyist podcasters, independent creators monetizing a single show, and larger media networks or businesses running multiple branded podcast channels under one account. Named competitors include Buzzsprout, Libsyn (Liberated Syndication), Spotify for Podcasters (formerly Anchor), Captivate, and Transistor. Podbean's combination of long operating history, integrated monetization, and unlimited storage on all paid tiers makes it a common recommendation for creators who want hosting and revenue tools without stitching together separate services for ads, memberships, and analytics.

features.title: One-click distribution
features.description: Publishes episodes automatically to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other major directories from a single dashboard.

features.title: Built-in monetization
features.description: Supports ads, listener subscriptions, premium paywalled episodes, and one-time donations without third-party tools.

features.title: Unlimited storage on paid plans
features.description: All paid tiers include unlimited total podcast storage and unmetered bandwidth.

features.title: AI-assisted production
features.description: Generates show notes, transcripts, and episode titles using an AI credit allowance included with each plan.

features.title: Custom podcast website and player
features.description: Provides a themeable podcast website and embeddable audio player for each show.

features.title: Advanced analytics
features.description: Tracks downloads, listener geography, and app breakdowns with IAB-certified stats on paid plans.

features.title: Live streaming
features.description: Allows creators to broadcast live audio sessions directly to listeners through the Podbean app.

features.title: Multi-podcast and team management
features.description: Higher tiers support multiple channels, team member roles, and network-level branding for organizations.

pros: Long operating history and financial stability as a bootstrapped, profitable company
Monetization tools (ads, Patron, premium content) built directly into the hosting platform
Unlimited storage and unmetered bandwidth on all paid plans
Competitive entry pricing compared to some hosting rivals
Mobile app supports recording, editing, and publishing from a phone
cons: Free plan storage and bandwidth limits are restrictive for active shows
Interface and website themes feel dated compared to newer hosting competitors
AI credits are capped per plan and can run out on heavier usage
Advanced monetization features often require reaching a minimum listener threshold
Video podcast hosting allowances are notably smaller than audio allowances

features: About 5 hours of total audio storage
Unlimited total storage and unmetered bandwidth

tools.name: GatherUp
tools.short_description: GatherUp is a customer feedback and online reputation management platform that helps local businesses and multi-location brands collect reviews, run NPS surveys and monitor their presence across 100-plus review sites.
tools.llm_readable_summary: GatherUp is a customer feedback, reviews and online reputation management platform aimed at local businesses, multi-location brands and the marketing agencies that serve them. Founded in San Jose, California in 2013 by a group of local search marketers, the company built its product around a straightforward but high-value workflow for local businesses: systematically ask customers for feedback right after a purchase or service interaction, route happy customers toward public reviews on sites like Google, and route unhappy customers into a private feedback loop the business can resolve before it becomes a public one-star review. In 2019, GatherUp was acquired by Traject, a marketing-technology holding company now headquartered in Austin, Texas, that also owns other local-marketing and social-media tools including Grade.us, Loomly, PLANOLY and Sendible. Under Traject's ownership, GatherUp has continued to operate as a distinct product and brand rather than being folded into a combined suite, and reported employee counts for the GatherUp team specifically range from roughly half a dozen to a few dozen depending on the data source, consistent with a mature, profitable product line inside a larger portfolio company rather than an independently scaling venture-funded startup. The core of GatherUp's platform is review and feedback generation: automated email and SMS requests sent to customers, configurable to route feedback based on sentiment, paired with monitoring across more than 100 review sites so a business can see, and via AI-assisted response suggestions reply to, new reviews as they appear across Google, Facebook and industry-specific directories without manually checking each site. Beyond basic review generation, GatherUp includes Net Promoter Score (NPS) survey tooling for measuring and tracking customer sentiment over time, QR codes and custom feedback links for capturing feedback in physical locations, review widgets and automated social sharing so positive reviews can be redistributed as marketing content, and API access plus customer-list upload integrations so the review-request workflow can be triggered from a business's existing CRM or point-of-sale system. Two notable paid add-ons extend the core platform: Listings Hub, which syncs a business's name, address and phone information across roughly 125 online directories to keep local SEO listings consistent, and Review Defense, a tiered service focused on detecting and disputing fraudulent or policy-violating reviews. GatherUp's pricing is subscription-based and scales with the number of business locations being managed. The entry-level Small Business plan is priced at 99 dollars per month for a single location and includes 300 SMS credits and 3,000 email credits per month, full 100-plus site review monitoring, NPS surveys and AI-generated review response suggestions; a 14-day free trial is commonly offered to let prospective customers evaluate the platform before committing. For businesses managing multiple locations, pricing drops on a per-location basis; reported figures show plans starting around 60 dollars per location per month for the 2-10 location tier, with further volume discounts available down to roughly 25 dollars per location per month for very large multi-location portfolios of 100-plus locations, reflecting the platform's dual focus on both single-location small businesses and large franchise or multi-brand operators. Agencies that want to resell reputation management services to their own clients under their own branding can access a separate white-label Agency plan, priced on a custom quote basis rather than published rate cards. GatherUp's competitive set spans several overlapping categories of reputation-management and customer-experience software. Birdeye and Podium are its most direct competitors, both offering broadly similar review generation, messaging and reputation-monitoring feature sets aimed at the same local-business and franchise market; Reputation.com competes more at the large-enterprise end of the market with heavier emphasis on social listening and broader brand reputation; and Yext competes primarily on the listings-management side that GatherUp addresses through its Listings Hub add-on. Within the Traject portfolio itself, Grade.us serves a similar reputation-management use case and is positioned more toward marketing agencies specifically, giving Traject two related but differently-targeted products in the same broad category. The realistic target audience for GatherUp spans single-location small businesses that want a straightforward, done-for-you review generation workflow; multi-location brands and franchise groups that need centralized reputation reporting across dozens or hundreds of locations with per-location cost efficiency; and marketing agencies that want to offer white-label reputation management as a service line to their own client base. Businesses primarily looking for the cheapest possible entry point may find GatherUp's 99 dollar per month starting price higher than some lighter-weight review-request tools, but the trade-off is a more complete feature set spanning surveys, listings and fraud-detection add-ons within a single connected platform.

features.title: Automated review generation
features.description: Sends email and SMS requests to customers, routing feedback based on sentiment toward public reviews or private resolution.

features.title: 100-plus review site monitoring
features.description: Tracks new reviews across major platforms and industry-specific directories from one dashboard.

features.title: NPS surveys
features.description: Measures and tracks Net Promoter Score and customer sentiment over time.

features.title: AI-generated review responses
features.description: Suggests AI-assisted replies to incoming reviews to save staff time.

features.title: Review widgets and social sharing
features.description: Automates redistribution of positive reviews as marketing content and website widgets.

features.title: QR codes and feedback links
features.description: Captures feedback in physical locations via custom links and QR codes.

features.title: Listings Hub add-on
features.description: Syncs business name, address and phone information across roughly 125 online directories.

features.title: Review Defense add-on
features.description: Detects and helps dispute fraudulent or policy-violating reviews.

pros: Broad review-site coverage across more than 100 platforms
AI-assisted review responses save time for busy teams
Flexible multi-location and white-label agency pricing options
Established player since 2013, backed by Traject's marketing-technology portfolio
Strong built-in reporting and NPS survey tooling
cons: Entry pricing of 99 dollars per month is high for a solo small business compared to some lighter competitors
Add-ons like Listings Hub and Review Defense cost extra on top of base plans
Per-location multi-site pricing can add up quickly for large franchise groups
Some pricing and plan details require a sales quote rather than being fully self-serve

features: 300 SMS and 3,000 email credits per month

tools.name: Grade.us
tools.short_description: Grade.us is a white-label review management and reputation marketing platform built for marketing agencies to generate, monitor, and market client reviews.
tools.llm_readable_summary:
Grade.us is a white-label online review management and reputation marketing platform purpose-built for marketing agencies, consultants, and SEOs to handle review generation, monitoring, and marketing on behalf of the client businesses they serve. Unlike consumer-facing or single-location review tools aimed at an individual brand managing its own reputation, Grade.us is architected from the ground up around the agency business model, where one operator manages review programs across many separate client accounts under their own brand.

The platform was founded in 2013 and operated independently for several years before becoming a subsidiary of Traject in 2020, a company that also owns a portfolio of other local-marketing and social-media SaaS tools. Grade.us is headquartered in Montclair, New Jersey, and continues to operate under its own brand as part of the Traject family of products.

Grade.us's central value proposition is white-labeling: agencies can fully rebrand the platform with their own logo, color scheme, and a unique custom domain, so that end clients interacting with review request emails, dashboards, and widgets see the agency's own brand rather than 'Grade.us' anywhere in the experience. This lets agencies resell review management as a distinct, billable service line to their existing client base, and the company markets the platform on the basis of the healthy profit margins agencies can realize by reselling it rather than building review-management tooling in-house.

On the feature side, Grade.us centers on a few core workflows. Automated review request campaigns are sent to a business's customers via both email and SMS text message, guiding satisfied customers toward leaving public reviews on the third-party sites that matter most for that business (Google, Facebook, and various industry-specific directories), while routing customers who indicate dissatisfaction into a private feedback and recovery flow instead of a public review page — a common 'review gating' pattern designed to protect public reputation while still capturing negative feedback for internal follow-up. Beyond generation, the platform provides centralized monitoring of reviews across dozens of third-party review sites in one dashboard, letting an agency track new reviews (including negative ones) and respond promptly across all of its clients from a single place. For marketing reviews once collected, Grade.us offers embeddable review widgets and a review carousel that clients can place on their own websites to display social proof and support local SEO, along with reporting and analytics tools agencies use to demonstrate the ROI of the service back to their clients.

Pricing follows a per-seat, tiered subscription model rather than a flat single price. The Partner plan starts at roughly $25 per seat per month, Agency at roughly $40 per seat per month, Professional at roughly $60 per seat per month, and Solo at roughly $110 per seat per month, with the exact tier a customer needs depending on the number of users and clients they intend to manage. SMS text message review request campaigns are billed as an add-on on top of the base plan, at roughly $10 per month per 200 messages. Grade.us offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required to start, and the company advertises that there are no long-term contracts, allowing customers to cancel at any time.

The platform's target audience is squarely digital marketing agencies and SEO consultancies that manage reputation and review generation for multiple small-business clients simultaneously, particularly in local-service verticals such as home services, healthcare practices, and retail. Company-published data cited by Grade.us notes that a large share of its user base comes from small companies, with a notable concentration of reviewers working in marketing and advertising roles, reinforcing its identity as an agency-first tool rather than a direct-to-business product.

Within the reputation and review management category, Grade.us competes with platforms like Birdeye, Podium, ReviewTrackers, and NiceJob, as well as broader local-marketing suites that bundle review management alongside other services. Its clearest differentiator is the agency-first, white-label business model: rather than selling directly to end businesses the way most competitors do, Grade.us is deliberately structured so that agencies can resell it under their own brand as a recurring revenue line, which in turn shapes its per-seat, multi-client pricing structure, its emphasis on client-facing reporting, and its overall positioning as infrastructure for building a reputation-management service line rather than a stand-alone consumer-facing product.

features.title: Automated email and SMS review requests
features.description: Sends review request campaigns to customers via email and text message to funnel them toward leaving public reviews.

features.title: Negative review recovery funnel
features.description: Routes dissatisfied customers into a private feedback flow instead of a public review page, protecting online reputation.

features.title: Multi-site review monitoring dashboard
features.description: Tracks and centralizes new reviews from dozens of third-party review sites in one place.

features.title: White-label branding and custom domain
features.description: Lets agencies rebrand the entire platform with their own logo and domain for client-facing use.

features.title: Review widget and carousel
features.description: Embeddable widgets display collected reviews on a client's website for SEO and social proof.

features.title: Client reporting and analytics
features.description: Provides reporting tools agencies can use to demonstrate review-management ROI to their clients.

features.title: Agency reseller model
features.description: Structured for agencies to resell review management as a billable service line under their own brand.

features.title: Major review site integrations
features.description: Connects with review platforms including Google and Facebook alongside industry-specific directories.

pros: Purpose-built white-label model lets agencies resell as their own product
Negative-review recovery funnel helps protect public reputation
Centralized multi-client dashboard saves agencies time managing many accounts
14-day free trial with no credit card required
No long-term contracts, with cancellation available at any time
cons: Per-seat pricing can add up for agencies managing many clients
SMS review request campaigns cost extra on top of the base plan
Pricing sits higher than some single-location review tools
Primarily agency-oriented, making it less ideal for a single independent business
Some users report price increases and a preference for annual billing

tools.name: NiceJob
tools.short_description: NiceJob is a reputation marketing platform that automates review requests, referrals, and social proof sharing to help local service businesses win more customers.
tools.llm_readable_summary: NiceJob is a reputation marketing and review management platform founded in 2017 and headquartered in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada, built to help local service businesses such as contractors, home service companies, salons, and franchises generate and showcase customer reviews. The platform automates the process of requesting reviews after a job or purchase, monitors reviews across platforms like Google and Facebook, and surfaces positive feedback through social proof widgets embedded on a business's website and a shareable microsite. In October 2021, NiceJob was acquired by Paystone, a Canadian payments and customer engagement company, and now operates as part of the Paystone family of products alongside payment processing and loyalty tools, while retaining its own brand and standalone product. NiceJob's core plans are the Reviews plan at 75 dollars per month, which covers automated review requests and follow-up reminders, multi-platform review monitoring, social proof widgets, AI-assisted review responses, staff leaderboards, and a branded microsite; and the Pro plan at 125 dollars per month, which adds automated booking reminders, referral campaigns, automated gifting for repeat customers, competitor SEO insight tracking, and NPS survey collection for deeper customer feedback. NiceJob also offers website-focused bundles: a Sites Only plan at 99 dollars per month (plus a 199 dollar setup fee) that includes a custom-built, SEO-optimized website with a guaranteed sales-lift promise, and a combined Grow plus Sites bundle at 174 dollars per month. All plans come with a 14-day free trial, no long-term contracts, and custom pricing for multi-location franchises. The company reports team sizes in the range of roughly 30 to 40 employees according to third-party data providers. NiceJob's target audience is small and mid-sized local service businesses -- particularly home services, contractors, salons, and franchises -- that depend heavily on online reviews and word-of-mouth referrals to win new customers. Its primary competitors in the reputation management and local marketing space include Birdeye, Podium, Grade.us, Broadly, and ReviewTrackers, with NiceJob differentiating itself through simplicity, an emphasis on 'nice' automated customer touchpoints (thank-you notes, gifting, referrals) rather than purely transactional review requests, and tight bundling with Paystone's payments ecosystem for businesses that already process payments through Paystone.

features.title: Automated review requests
features.description: Sends timed text and email requests asking recent customers to leave a review, with automated follow-up reminders to increase response rates.

features.title: Multi-platform review monitoring
features.description: Tracks new reviews across Google, Facebook, and other major platforms from a single dashboard so businesses never miss feedback.

features.title: Social proof widgets
features.description: Automatically displays recent positive reviews on the business's website to build trust with prospective customers.

features.title: Branded microsite
features.description: Generates a shareable microsite that showcases a business's best reviews, useful for social media and marketing campaigns.

features.title: Referral automation
features.description: Encourages happy customers to refer new business through automated, trackable referral campaigns (available on Pro plan).

features.title: AI-assisted review responses
features.description: Uses AI to help draft timely responses to customer reviews, reducing the manual effort of reputation management.

features.title: NPS surveys
features.description: Collects Net Promoter Score feedback to identify at-risk customers and advocates beyond simple star ratings (Pro plan).

features.title: Staff leaderboards
features.description: Gamifies review generation by ranking staff members on review volume and ratings to encourage friendly competition.

pros: Purpose-built for local service businesses with straightforward setup
Combines review generation with referral and gifting automation, not just review requests
Includes social proof widgets and a shareable microsite out of the box
14-day free trial with no long-term contract required
Backed by Paystone's broader payments and customer engagement ecosystem
cons: Pricing is higher than some basic review-request-only tools
Website bundle plans carry an additional one-time setup fee
Advanced features like referrals and NPS surveys are locked behind the higher Pro tier
Primarily focused on English-language markets and North American review platforms
Multi-location and franchise pricing requires a custom quote rather than transparent published rates

tools.name: ActivityWatch
tools.short_description: Free, open-source, privacy-first time-tracking app that automatically logs which apps and websites you use, storing all data locally on your own device with no cloud sync required.
tools.llm_readable_summary: ActivityWatch is a free, open-source application that automatically tracks how users spend time on their computers and phones, logging active applications, window titles, and (via browser extensions) websites visited. It was created by brothers Erik Bjäreholt and Johan Bjäreholt, who continue to maintain it, built because they wanted this kind of tool and it didn't exist yet; it's now maintained with community contributions. The software is licensed under MPL-2.0 and explicitly has 'no premium tiers, no usage limits, and no ads' — it is entirely free rather than freemium. Its defining feature is a privacy-first, local-first architecture: all tracked time data is stored on the user's own device and, according to the project, is never sent to any server, giving users full control over their data. Core capabilities include automatic tracking of active applications and window titles, category rules for organizing activity into groups, an activity timeline and dashboard for visualization, browser extensions for Chrome and Firefox that track website visits, and editor plugins for tracking coding time. Cross-device synchronization is listed as in development rather than fully mature. ActivityWatch runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android (no official iOS app was found). It appeals to individuals who want personal productivity or digital-wellbeing insight, and to developers or privacy-conscious users and freelancers who want time-tracking data that never leaves their own machine.

features.title: Automatic app & window tracking
features.description: Logs active applications and window titles in the background without manual input.

features.title: Browser extensions
features.description: Chrome and Firefox extensions extend tracking to website visits.

features.title: Editor plugins
features.description: Plugins for tracking time spent coding directly in supported editors.

features.title: Local-first storage
features.description: All data is stored on-device and never sent to a server.

features.title: Category rules
features.description: Group raw activity data into custom categories for easier review.

features.title: Activity dashboard
features.description: Local timeline and dashboard for visualizing tracked time.

pros: Completely free with no premium tiers, usage limits, or ads
Strong privacy model — data never leaves the user's device
Open source (MPL-2.0) and extensible
Cross-platform: Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android
cons: No official iOS app
Cross-device data synchronization is still in development
As a volunteer-maintained project, support and integrations depend on community contribution rather than a commercial team

tools.name: Kimai
tools.short_description: Kimai is an open source time-tracking application for freelancers and teams, free to self-host or available as a hosted Kimai Cloud subscription.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Kimai is an open-source, web-based time-tracking application whose roots trace back to 2006, when developer Torsten Hoeltge's team began building it as a simple PHP timesheet tool. In 2009, when Torsten no longer had time to continue the project, Vienna-based developer Kevin Papst took over its development, eventually rewriting the codebase into what became known as 'KimaiNext' (now simply the current Kimai) and growing it from a side project into his full-time work, operating today as an independent open-source maintainer and small company based in Vienna, Austria. The software is licensed under the AGPL, so the core self-hosted product is completely free to download, install, and run on a company's own server or via Docker, with no user limits and no feature paywall in the open-source edition itself; Kimai describes itself as a leading open-source time-tracking application, used by freelancers, agencies, and companies to log hours, manage timesheets, and generate client invoices without spreadsheets or manual rounding. Functionally, Kimai organizes tracked time against a hierarchy of customers, projects, and activities, so that hours logged by any number of users roll up cleanly into project- and client-level reporting; at invoicing time, bills are generated directly from actual recorded hours rather than estimates. The platform includes an extensive JSON API for reading and writing timesheet data so it can be embedded into other internal tools, native support for two-factor authentication (TOTP), and single sign-on via external identity providers including LDAP and SAML integrations with providers like Google Workspace and Azure AD, plus a marketplace of official plugins that add advanced invoicing templates, custom reporting, multi-step approval workflows, and expense tracking on top of the free core. For teams that don't want to run their own server, Kimai also operates Kimai Cloud, a fully managed SaaS version hosted on servers in Germany for GDPR compliance, offered on two paid subscription tiers: a Standard plan priced at EUR 2.99 per user per month when billed annually (EUR 3.99 monthly) that includes project time tracking, billable and non-billable hour tracking, invoicing, data export, audit logs, and email support; and a Professional plan at EUR 3.99 per user per month annually (EUR 4.99 monthly) that adds working-hour accounts, public-holiday, vacation, and sick-leave tracking, expense tracking, custom fields, task management, a client-facing customer portal, kiosk mode for shared time-clock devices, daily backups, SAML single sign-on, and a custom domain with SSL. Kimai Cloud also offers a discounted rate of EUR 0.99 per user per month (billed annually) for non-profits and select industries, and every cloud plan includes a 30-day free trial with no setup fees and month-to-month cancellation. The target audience spans the full range from individual freelancers tracking billable hours for a handful of clients up to mid-size agencies and companies needing multi-user timesheets, approval chains, and automated invoicing, with the self-hosted option appealing especially to organizations with data-residency or budget constraints that want to avoid recurring per-seat SaaS costs, and Kimai Cloud appealing to teams that want the same feature set without operating their own server. Within the time-tracking software category, Kimai's most direct competitors are commercial SaaS tools such as Toggl Track, Clockify, Harvest, and TimeCamp, none of which offer a comparably mature, freely self-hostable open-source core; Kimai differentiates itself primarily on that open-source, data-ownership angle combined with genuinely low per-seat SaaS pricing on Kimai Cloud when self-hosting isn't desired, an extensive plugin ecosystem, and deep customization capability through its open API and codebase that closed competitors don't expose.

features.title: Time tracking by customer, project, and activity
features.description: Organizes logged hours into a clear hierarchy for accurate reporting and invoicing.

features.title: Automated invoicing
features.description: Generates client invoices directly from recorded billable hours.

features.title: JSON API
features.description: Extensive API for reading and writing timesheet data to integrate with other tools.

features.title: Two-factor authentication and SSO
features.description: TOTP-based 2FA plus LDAP and SAML single sign-on support.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: Official plugins add advanced invoicing, custom reports, approval workflows, and expense tracking.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Unlimited users with role-based permissions in self-hosted installations.

features.title: Kimai Cloud hosting
features.description: Fully managed SaaS option hosted in Germany with GDPR compliance.

features.title: Kiosk mode and customer portal
features.description: Shared time-clock device mode and a client-facing portal on the Professional plan.

pros: Free, self-hostable open-source core with no user limits
Low per-seat pricing on Kimai Cloud compared to many SaaS competitors
Extensive JSON API and plugin ecosystem for customization
GDPR-compliant hosting option in Germany
Active development with a long project history since 2006
cons: Self-hosting requires server setup and maintenance effort
Advanced features like approvals and expense tracking require the Professional plan or plugins
Smaller company behind the product compared to large SaaS time-tracking vendors
Interface may feel less polished than premium commercial competitors
Non-profit discount pricing requires annual billing

features: Working hour accounts and leave tracking

tools.name: Solidtime
tools.short_description: Solidtime is an open-source time tracking app for freelancers and teams, available as a free self-hosted deployment or a paid managed cloud plan.
tools.llm_readable_summary: Solidtime is an open-source time tracking application built for freelancers, agencies, and teams that want a modern alternative to expensive, closed-source SaaS time trackers such as Toggl or Harvest. Founded around 2024 and based in Vienna, Austria, Solidtime is released under the AGPL-3.0 license, with the full source code available on GitHub, meaning it can be self-hosted for free indefinitely. The company also offers a managed cloud version with a Solo plan that is free for individual freelancers and a Professional plan priced at 8 euros per user per month when billed annually, or 9 euros per user per month billed monthly. The Professional plan adds team access management, invoicing, project and task estimates, time entry rounding, and PDF or shareable reports. Core features available across plans include time entry tracking, clients, projects, tags, tasks, billable hours and rates, and reporting. A separate on-premise pricing structure exists for organizations that want to self-host with a commercial license and support beyond the free open-source option. Solidtime is best suited for freelancers who bill clients by the hour, small agencies managing multiple client projects, and privacy-conscious teams that prefer to self-host rather than rely on a third-party SaaS provider.

features.title: Time Entry Tracking
features.description: Log billable and non-billable time against clients, projects, and tasks with a simple, modern interface.

features.title: Client and Project Management
features.description: Organize work by client and project, with tags for further categorization and filtering.

features.title: Billable Rates
features.description: Set billable rates per client, project, or user to generate accurate invoices from tracked time.

features.title: Team Access Management
features.description: Manage roles and permissions at the project and organization level on the Professional plan.

features.title: Invoicing
features.description: Generate invoices directly from tracked billable time on the Professional plan.

features.title: Reporting and Exports
features.description: Create PDF and shareable reports to summarize time spent by client, project, or team member.

features.title: Self-Hosting
features.description: Deploy Solidtime on your own infrastructure for free using the open-source AGPL-3.0 licensed code.

features.title: Project and Task Estimates
features.description: Set time estimates for projects and tasks to track progress against budgeted hours on the Professional plan.

pros: Free and open source with the option to self-host indefinitely at no cost
Modern, clean interface compared to many legacy time tracking tools
Transparent, low-cost paid tier for teams that want a managed cloud version
Built-in invoicing and billable rate tracking suited to freelancers and agencies
cons: Newer project, founded around 2024, with a smaller feature set and community than established competitors like Toggl or Harvest
Self-hosting requires technical setup and ongoing maintenance
Fewer third-party integrations compared to mature commercial time trackers
Company size and long-term backing are not well documented, which may concern risk-averse enterprise buyers

features: Team access management at project and organization level
Unlimited users on your own infrastructure
